details.text: Derivative: وَعِيدِ (Wa’eed) is a threatening promise related to a punishment or something negative happening in the future.

Derivative: جَبَّارٍ (Jabbaar) comes from جَبَرَ – يَجْبُرُ – جَبْر which means to force and compel. جَبَّارٍ (Jabbaar) refers to the arrogant, conceited person who looks down on others, claiming a status or something that is not their right or forces his will upon others.

Derivative: عَنِيدٍ (‘Aneed) comes from عَنَدَ – عَنَاد and means “Obstinate or stubborn”. It refers to someone who sticks to his opinion without flexibility or budging. It implies insisting upon one’s position or view even when proven wrong.
Derivative: صَدِيدٍ (Sadeed) refers to pus that emits from a wound and infection. It refers to a putrid foul liquid in hellfire which will be given as drink.
Derivative: يُسِيغُ is from أَسَاغَ and means “to swallow and go down the throat smoothly or with ease”.
Derivative: يَتَجَرَّعُ comes from تَجَرَّعَ – تَجَرُّعًا which means to drink or swallow something gulp by gulp because it is difficult to drink in one go.
Derivative: عَزِيزٍ (‘Azeez) comes from عَزَّ and one of its meanings which is to be “difficult”.

Derivative: جَزِعْنَا is from جَزِعَ which implies the opposite of صَبَرَ which means “To remain patient and persevere”. جَزِعَ means to be anxious and overwhelmed whereby the person cannot remain upon his course.

Derivative: مَّحِيصٍ (Mahees) is from the root letters ح ي ص from the verb حَاصَ – يَحِيصُ – حَيْصًا which means to “flee or escape”. مَّحِيصٍ (Mahees) refers to “a place where one can escape or flee to” i.e., a refuge or sanctuary.
Derivative: مُصْرِخ (Musrikh) carries the meaning of “helping and coming to someone’s aid”.

Derivative: خِلَالٌ (Khilaal) is the مَصْدَر (Masdar – Verbal noun) from خَالَلَ which means “to befriend”. It can also be the plural of خُلَّة (Khullah) which means “friendship” or the plural of خَلِيل (Khaleel) which means “a close friend”. خِلَالٌ (Khilaal) therefore refers to “friendship” or “friends”.

Derivative: سَخَّرَ - تَسْخِير means the act of making something subdued, tamed, pliable and capable of being controlled and utilized or benefited from. It implies subduing something for the service of others.

Derivative: دَائِبَيْنِ is the dual form of دَائِب (Daa’ib) which is from the verb دَأَبَ – يَدْأَبُ – دَأْب which means “to persevere, apply oneself or work tirelessly” in something without taking a break.

Derivative: كَفَّارٌ (Kaffaar) is a form of مُبَاَلَغَة (Mubaalaghah – Hyperbole) which implies repetition. كَفَّارٌ (Kaffaar) refers to the one who is “repeatedly ungrateful or engaged in disbelief”.
Derivative: سَأَلْتُم – سَأَلَ means “to ask” and includes within its meaning here that which is intrinsically demanded and asked for without being vocalised, like the needs of the human being.

Derivative: ظَلُومٌ (Zhaloom) is the مُبَاَلَغَة (Mubaalaghah – Hyperbole) form of the اِسْمُ الفَاعِل (Ism ul-Faa’il – Active participle) ظَالِم (Zhaalim) which means “a transgressor, wrongdoer or oppressor” or “transgressing, wrongdoing and oppressive”. ظَلُومٌ (Zhaloom) therefore implies the meaning of “extreme/excessive wrongdoer” or “extremely/excessively wrongdoing”.
Derivative: الْأَصْنَامَ (Asnaam) is the plural of صَنَمٌ (Sanam) and refers to portable idols which are made from wood or metals.
Derivative: اجْنُبْ is the command from جَنَبَ – يَجْنُبُ which means “to ward off and keep away”.
Derivative: عَصَا / عَصَى – يَعْصِي – عِصْيان means “to disobey”.

- “No doubt they (إِنَّهُنَّ), they have led astray (أَضْلَلْنَ)” – This is in reference to the أَصْنَام (Asnaam – Carved or sculptured idols) which are non-human, however, the human plural form is used instead of the non-human form. This reflects a style which is used to magnify the issue of what is being spoken about.
Derivative: تَهْوِي comes from هَوَى – يَهْوِي means “to fall” from a height to somewhere low. It also means to incline towards and to have an affection or yearning for someone or something.
Derivative: تَشْخَصُ comes from شَخَصَ – يَشْخَصُ – شُخُوص which means for eyes “to be fixed and set” i.e., they won’t move and will be fixed due to being petrified and in a state of terror.

Derivative: مُهْطِعِينَ is the plural of مُهْطِع (Muhti’) which is the اِسمُ الفَاعِل (Ism ul-Faa’il – Active participle) from أَهْطَعَ – يُهْطِعُ – إِهْطَاع which means “to move fast” towards something i.e., to rush. It has also been said to mean “staring or keeping your sight fixed on something continuously”.
Derivative: مُقْنِعِي here means “lifting or raising”.
Derivative: هَوَاءٌ (Hawaa’) means “air” and implies the meaning of being empty and void, or the meaning of fluttering around whilst not finding a place to settle in.
Derivative: زَوَال (Zawaal) comes from زَالَ – يَزُولُ and it means “disappearance”, “vanishing” or “end”.

“The possessor of (ذُو) retribution or vengeance (انتِقَامٍ)” - ذُو انتِقَامٍ (Dhu Intiqaam) is stronger linguistically than the meaning of مُنْتَقِم (Muntaqim) which refers to the one who “takes vengeance” in the sense that it describes the power and capability to take vengeance and deliver retribution.

Derivative: الْأَصْفَادِ (Asfaad) is the plural of صَفَد (Safad) which refers to something used to bond or tie like a shackle or fetter like handcuffs or rope for example. It comes from صَفَدَ which means “to tie someone up, bind or shackle them”.

Derivative: مُّقَرَّنِينَ is the plural of مُقَرَّن (Muqarran) which is the اِسمُ المَفْعُول (Ism ul-Maf’ool – Passive participle) of the verb قَرَّنَ which means to “tie up or bind” together. مُقَرَّن (Muqarran) means to be “tied up or bound” together i.e., with chains or shackles.

Derivative: قَطِرَانٍ (Qatiraan) has been said to refer to molten (i.e., liquified) copper but the majority have stated that it refers to liquid tar type substance i.e., a thick, sticky and highly inflammable substance that has a horrible smell.
Derivative: سَرَابِيلُ (Saraabeel) is the plural of سِرْبَال (Sirbaal) which refers to clothing and a shirt or garment that is worn.
Derivative: بَلَاغٌ (Balaagh) comes from the verb بَلَغَ which means “to reach”. بَلَاغٌ (Balaagh) refers to far-reaching speech or communication that is conveyed far and wide.
Derivative: غَنِىٌّ (Ghaniy) refers to the One who is free of all need and is need of nobody or nothing else.
Derivative: حَمِيدٌ (Hameed) is the One who is praised in and of Himself without the need of anyone or anything to praise Him for His praise to exist.

- “They returned or put (رَدُّوا) their hands (أَيْدِيَهُمْ) in their mouths (فِي أَفْوَاهِهِمْ)”. It has been said that it means that they placed their hands in their mouths to bite their fingers out of rage and anger towards the Messengers that came to them. The expression implies their immense frustration and agitation with the Messengers.
